1:
"When a man hath taken a wife, and married her, and it come to pass that she find no favour in his eyes, because he hath found some uncleanness in her: then let him write her a bill of divorcement, and give it in her hand, and send her out of his house." The witnesses note the law of the bill of divorcement — the crux of the regulation, not the institution, of divorce, and its place under the gospel. On "let him write her a bill of divorcement, and give it in her hand, and send her out of his house," interpreters expound the law. The grammatical-historical Keil and the dominant reading note the regulation — "when a man hath taken a wife... and it come to pass that she find no favour in his eyes, because he hath found some uncleanness in her: then let him write her a bill of divorcement... and send her out of his house": the law did not institute divorce but regulated and restrained an existing practice; the witnesses note the significance — the law tolerated and regulated divorce because of the hardness of men's hearts, not as God's original design; the lesson — that this was a concession to human hardness, not the will of God; the truth that Christ declared this a permission for the hardness of heart, not the creation ordinance, "Moses because of the hardness of your hearts suffered you to put away your wives: but from the beginning it was not so," Matthew 19:8; the figure — the contrast between the law's concession and the gospel's restoration of marriage to its first design, "what therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder," Matthew 19:6; the warning against the hardness of heart that breaks the marriage bond, the call back to God's design. The figure of the regulation of divorce as a concession to hardness of heart, fulfilled in the gospel's restoration of marriage.

On "when a man hath taken a wife, and married her... because he hath found some uncleanness in her," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the ground. Calvin and Gill note the disputed "uncleanness," the ground of the divorce, variously understood, the occasion of the practice the law regulated (the hardness of heart, Matthew 19:8) — the ground (some uncleanness).

On "then let him write her a bill of divorcement, and give it in her hand, and send her out of his house," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the bill. Ellicott and Keil note the formal bill of divorcement, a restraint on hasty putting-away and a protection of the woman, the practice regulated not instituted (the concession restrained, Matthew 19:6) — the figure (the gospel restoring marriage to its first design); the providence (the law, the divorced woman not to return to follow, vv.2-4). The verse displays the law of the bill of divorcement.

The witnesses agree the substance: the law did not institute but regulated and restrained divorce, a concession to hardness of heart (Keil, Calvin, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the regulation of divorce: the bill of divorcement — not God's design but a tolerated and restrained practice, a permission for the hardness of heart and not the creation ordinance (Matthew 19:8), fulfilled in the gospel's restoration of marriage to its first indissoluble design (Matthew 19:6). The verse displays the divorce-regulation crux. The law of the bill of divorcement displays the crux: let him write her a bill of divorcement... and send her out of his house (Matthew 19:6; 19:8) — the law regulating and restraining an existing practice, fulfilled in the gospel's call back to the indissoluble union "from the beginning." The verse displays that the divorced and remarried woman might not return to her first husband (vv.2-4).
2:
"And when she is departed out of his house, she may go and be another man's wife." The very brief verse notes that the divorced woman might marry another. The grammatical voices note the remarriage. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when she is departed out of his house, she may go and be another man's wife" — the divorced woman was free to marry another man; the and when she is departed out of his house (the departure); the she may go and be another man's wife (the remarriage); the figure (the divorced woman free to remarry, the practice the law regulated; the truth that the bill of divorce freed her to marry again; the figure of the toleration that the law allowed, the remarriage permitted; the practice the law restrained but did not forbid, the woman free; the regulation that led to the prohibition of return, vv.4). The verse holds together the remarriage of the divorced.

On "And when she is departed out of his house," the witnesses note the departure. Gill notes the woman departed from the house (the divorce) — the departure (out of the house).

On "she may go and be another man's wife," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the remarriage: "she may go and be another man's wife" — the divorced woman free to remarry (the toleration); the figure (the practice regulated); the providence (the remarriage, the prohibition of return to follow, vv.3-4). The verse displays that the divorced woman might marry another.

The verse displays the remarriage. The verse marks the woman: departed. The verse displays the remarriage: allowed. The verse displays the practice: tolerated. The verse displays the case of the second husband also putting her away or dying (vv.3-4).
3:
"And if the latter husband hate her, and write her a bill of divorcement, and giveth it in her hand, and sendeth her out of his house; or if the latter husband die, which took her to be his wife;" The very brief verse notes the case of the second husband divorcing her or dying. The grammatical voices note the case. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "if the latter husband hate her, and write her a bill of divorcement... or if the latter husband die" — the case is put of the second marriage ending by divorce or death; the and if the latter husband hate her, and write her a bill of divorcement (the second divorce); the and giveth it in her hand, and sendeth her out of his house (the putting away); the or if the latter husband die, which took her to be his wife (or the death); the figure (the second marriage ending, the case that leads to the prohibition; the truth that whether by divorce or death, the second union ending, the woman might not return to the first; the figure of the case defined, the ground of the prohibition to follow; the second husband's divorce or death, the woman free again; the case that grounds the law of vv.4). The verse holds together the case of the second marriage ending.

On "or if the latter husband die, which took her to be his wife," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the death: "or if the latter husband die, which took her to be his wife" — the second husband's death (the union ended); the figure (the case grounding the prohibition); the providence (the case, the prohibition of return to the first husband to follow, vv.4). The verse displays the case of the second husband divorcing her or dying.

4:
"Her former husband, which sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife, after that she is defiled; for that is abomination before the LORD: and thou shalt not cause the land to sin, which the LORD thy God giveth thee for an inheritance." The very brief verse notes that the first husband might not take her again after she was defiled. The grammatical voices note the prohibition. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "her former husband, which sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife, after that she is defiled; for that is abomination before the LORD" — the first husband was forbidden to remarry the woman after her second marriage; the her former husband, which sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife (the prohibition of return); the after that she is defiled (after the second marriage); the for that is abomination before the LORD (the abomination); the and thou shalt not cause the land to sin (the land not to be defiled); the figure (the prohibition of return, the marriage not to be lightly broken and resumed; the truth that the bond once broken and another formed could not be resumed, lest marriage be made light of; the figure of the restraint on hasty divorce, the prohibition a check on caprice; the dignity of marriage guarded, the land not to be defiled; the abomination that this restraint forbade, Jeremiah 3:1). The verse holds together the prohibition of return.

On "Her former husband, which sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife, after that she is defiled; for that is abomination before the LORD," the witnesses note the prohibition. Gill notes the first husband forbidden to remarry her (the abomination, Jeremiah 3:1) — the prohibition (no return).

5:
"When a man hath taken a new wife, he shall not go out to war, neither shall he be charged with any business: but he shall be free at home one year, and shall cheer up his wife which he hath taken." The very brief verse notes the newly married man free from war for a year to cheer his wife. The grammatical voices note the exemption. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when a man hath taken a new wife, he shall not go out to war... but he shall be free at home one year, and shall cheer up his wife" — the newly married man was exempt from war and public duty for a year; the when a man hath taken a new wife, he shall not go out to war (exempt from war); the neither shall he be charged with any business (free of public duty); the but he shall be free at home one year, and shall cheer up his wife which he hath taken (free to gladden his wife); the figure (the kindness of the law to the newly married, the first year free to establish the home; the truth that the law cherished the marriage bond, the husband free to gladden his wife; the figure of the tenderness of God's law toward marriage and the home, the joy of the first year guarded; the care for the new household, the wife to be cheered; the gentleness of the law that values the home, vv.4). The verse holds together the exemption of the newly married.

6:
"No man shall take the nether or the upper millstone to pledge: for he taketh a man's life to pledge." The very brief verse notes that the millstone was not to be taken in pledge. The grammatical voices note the pledge. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "no man shall take the nether or the upper millstone to pledge: for he taketh a man's life to pledge" — the millstone, by which the family ground their daily bread, was not to be taken as a pledge; the no man shall take the nether or the upper millstone to pledge (the millstone not taken); the for he taketh a man's life to pledge (the means of life); the figure (the prohibition of taking the means of livelihood in pledge, the mercy of the law; the truth that to take the millstone was to take the family's daily bread, their very life; the figure of the law's care for the poor, the necessities of life not to be seized; the mercy that forbids depriving a man of his means of living; the protection of the household's sustenance, vv.10-13). The verse holds together the millstone not taken in pledge.

7:
"If a man be found stealing any of his brethren of the children of Israel, and maketh merchandise of him, or selleth him; then that thief shall die; and thou shalt put evil away from among you." The very brief verse notes the man-stealer to be put to death. The grammatical voices note the judgment. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "if a man be found stealing any of his brethren... and maketh merchandise of him, or selleth him; then that thief shall die" — the man-stealer, who kidnapped and sold a fellow Israelite, was to die; the if a man be found stealing any of his brethren of the children of Israel (the kidnapping); the and maketh merchandise of him, or selleth him (the selling); the then that thief shall die; and thou shalt put evil away from among you (the death and purging); the figure (the man-stealer judged with death, the gravity of the crime; the truth that to steal and sell a man was a capital crime, the evil purged; the figure of the sanctity of human freedom, the kidnapper condemned, "menstealers" among the lawless, 1 Timothy 1:10; the gravity of trafficking in men, the death penalty; the evil rooted out, the freedom of the person guarded, vv.6). The verse holds together the man-stealer's death.

8:
"Take heed in the plague of leprosy, that thou observe diligently, and do according to all that the priests the Levites shall teach you: as I commanded them, so ye shall observe to do." 10:

16:
"The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, neither shall the children be put to death for the fathers: every man shall be put to death for his own sin." The witnesses note the law that each man is to die for his own sin, not the fathers for the children nor the children for the fathers — the crux of individual responsibility in judgment. On "the fathers shall not be put to death for the children... every man shall be put to death for his own sin," interpreters expound the law. The grammatical-historical Keil and the dominant reading note individual responsibility — "the fathers shall not be put to death for the children, neither shall the children be put to death for the fathers: every man shall be put to death for his own sin": in human justice, none was to be executed for another's crime, but each for his own; the witnesses note the significance — the principle of individual responsibility in the administration of justice; the lesson — that justice is personal, the guilty alone to suffer, the innocent not punished for another; the truth that this principle, here given to the judges, was taken up by the prophets against the proverb that the children's teeth were set on edge by the fathers' grapes, "the soul that sinneth, it shall die," Ezekiel 18:20; "every one shall die for his own iniquity," Jeremiah 31:30; the figure — the personal accountability of each soul before God, every man to give account of himself, "so then every one of us shall give account of himself to God," Romans 14:12; the distinction between this rule for human courts and the gospel's one exception, the willing substitution of Christ who bore the sins of others. The figure of individual responsibility, each man answerable for his own sin.

On "the fathers shall not be put to death for the children, neither shall the children be put to death for the fathers," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the rule. 22:
"And thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in the land of Egypt: therefore I command thee to do this thing." The very brief verse notes the remembrance of bondage as the motive for charity. The grammatical voices note the motive. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in the land of Egypt: therefore I command thee to do this thing" — the memory of their bondage was the ground of the charity commanded; the and thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in the land of Egypt (the memory of bondage); the therefore I command thee to do this thing (the motive for charity); the figure (the remembrance of their own slavery the spring of charity to the poor; the truth that those once in bondage should remember the poor and stranger; the figure of the gratitude that breeds generosity, the redeemed merciful; the memory of God's mercy the motive for mercy to others, vv.18; the charity grounded on the remembrance of their own need). The verse closes the chapter, the laws of charity.

On "And thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in the land of Egypt," the witnesses note the remembrance. Gill notes the memory of bondage (the motive) — the remembrance (a bondman).

On "therefore I command thee to do this thing," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the motive: "therefore I command thee to do this thing" — the charity grounded on the remembrance (the gratitude that breeds generosity); the figure (the redeemed merciful to the poor); the providence (the motive, the law of stripes to follow, ch.25). The verse displays the remembrance of bondage as the motive for charity.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the motive — "thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in the land of Egypt: therefore I command thee to do this thing," the motive (Keil, Gill); "And thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in the land of Egypt," the remembrance (Gill); "therefore I command thee to do this thing," the motive (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the motive. The verse marks the bondage: remembered. The verse displays the charity: commanded. The verse displays the gratitude: the spring. The verse displays the mercy: required. The motive displays the remembrance that breeds generosity. The verse displays the slavery: remembered. The verse displays the charity: grounded. The verse displays the generosity: required. The remembrance of bondage as the motive for charity displays the gratitude that breeds generosity: thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in the land of Egypt: therefore I command thee to do this thing — the memory of their own slavery the ground of the charity commanded, those once in bondage to remember the poor and stranger. The verse closes the chapter, before the law of stripes and the levirate (ch.25).
